graph.name: Sunday Sky Personalized Video Rendering as a Service (RAS)
graph.notes: Sunday Sky provides a 'Rendering as a Service' (RAS) for personalized video experiences, such as dynamic telco bills. The architecture is designed to handle billions of videos yearly and millions daily. Key design decisions include a serverless frontend (API Gateway, Lambda), an asynchronous rendering backend using SQS queues for decoupling, and EC2 Auto Scaling Groups running on cost-efficient Spot Instances. High availability is achieved through multi-AZ deployment and diversified instance types within the ASG. A sophisticated mechanism handles Spot Instance interruptions gracefully, draining jobs before termination. Scaling is managed by a custom solution using CloudWatch metrics, Step Functions, and Lambda, dynamically adjusting ASG capacity to meet demand.

nodes.name: User (Mobile/PC)
nodes.notes: User accessing personalized video content from a mobile device or PC.

nodes.name: API Gateway
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Front-end for the RAS service; configures usage plans and API keys for customer load control.

nodes.name: RAS Service Frontend
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Performs validations on rendering requests; pushes rendering instructions to SQS; pulls rendering job responses from SQS; returns playlist links to users.

nodes.name: Rendering Instruction Queue
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Stores rendering instructions pushed by the Lambda frontend, decoupling the request from the rendering engines. WORKLOAD_PEEK: Engines pull from this queue.

nodes.name: Rendering Engine ASG
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Runs the core rendering engine services, pulling jobs from SQS. Utilizes spot instances across multiple availability zones with diversified instance types for high availability and cost efficiency. DATA_PEEK: Uploads personalized video segments to S3.

nodes.name: Rendering Response Queue
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Provides a quick response channel for rendering engines to communicate status/completion back to the Lambda frontend.

nodes.name: Video Segments Storage
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Stores personalized video segments (e.g., HLS/DASH chunks) uploaded by the rendering engines, acting as the origin for CloudFront.

nodes.name: CloudFront Distribution
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Caches and delivers personalized video segments to users from the S3 origin, ensuring low latency playback.

nodes.name: CloudWatch Events (Spot Interruption)
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Notifies the Spot Interruption Handler Lambda function of impending two-minute spot instance termination notices.

nodes.name: Spot Interruption Handler
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Triggered by CloudWatch Events. Interacts with the specific instance about to be terminated via an API call, instructing it to stop pulling new rendering requests and drain current jobs. Subsequently detaches the instance from the Auto Scaling Group.

nodes.name: CloudWatch Metrics
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Collects performance and utilization metrics from the rendering instances. WORKLOAD_PEEK: Metrics are consumed by the Auto Scaling Controller Lambda.

nodes.name: Scaling Orchestrator
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Triggers the Auto Scaling Controller Lambda function every 15 seconds to evaluate and adjust scaling.

nodes.name: Auto Scaling Controller
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Triggered by Step Functions. Consumes CloudWatch metrics and calculates expected traffic to interact with the EC2 Auto Scaling Group, adjusting the number of instances in service. FUTURE_WORK: Will integrate with ASG's weighted capacity feature for better representation of rendering capacity.

edges.notes: User requests personalized video content from their device.
API Gateway forwards the rendering request to the RAS Service Frontend Lambda.
RAS Service Frontend Lambda pushes rendering instructions to the SQS queue.
Rendering engines in the EC2 Auto Scaling Group pull rendering jobs from the SQS queue when capacity is available.
Upon receiving a job, the rendering engine immediately sends a quick response (e.g., job ID) to the SQS response queue.
The RAS Service Frontend Lambda pulls rendering responses from the SQS response queue.
The rendering engine uploads generated video segments to S3.
The RAS Service Frontend Lambda returns a playlist file link (containing CloudFront URLs) to the user's device.
The user's video player requests video segments from CloudFront using the playlist link.
CloudFront pulls video segments from S3 (the origin).
CloudFront delivers the video segments to the user's device.
CloudWatch Events notifies the Spot Interruption Handler Lambda of an impending spot instance termination.
The Lambda function interacts with the specific instance within the ASG to stop pulling new jobs and drain existing ones via an API call.
The Lambda function detaches the interrupted instance from the Auto Scaling Group.
Rendering instances push performance and utilization metrics to CloudWatch.
Step Functions triggers the Auto Scaling Controller Lambda every 15 seconds.
The Auto Scaling Controller Lambda consumes CloudWatch metrics.
Based on metrics, the Lambda function instructs the EC2 Auto Scaling Group to increase or decrease the number of instances.
